text: Robotic transabdominal preperitoneal epigastric hernia repair. Our patient is a fifty nine year old female with a painful and enlarging recurrent epigastric slash incisional hernia. She complained of pain at the site and progressive bulging. Past medical history is significant for diabetes, hypertension and obesity with a BMI of thirty one. Her past surgical history is significant for an open cholecystectomy in the remote past and an open incisional hernia repair with placement of an intraperitoneal mesh. CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis was performed, which reveals a small epigastric recurrence containing fat. She also has a small umbilical hernia.

A 12mm trocar is placed through the umbilical hernia defect, followed by the placement of 8mm trocars spaced 10 centimeters apart. The camera is positioned 15 centimeters away from the defect's inferior aspect to facilitate incision of the peritoneum for mesh overlap. The robotic system is docked over the patient's shoulder, and instruments including ProGrasp, monopolar scissors, and a suture cut needle driver are utilized. The procedure begins with lysis of adhesions, reduction of hernia content, and clearance of the anterior abdominal wall.

The surgeon observes that the colon is adhered to the anterior abdominal wall. Old mesh from a previous operation is visible, and a decision is made to explant the mesh, which may create a peritoneal defect.

The surgeon makes a peritoneal incision at least five centimeters below the inferior aspect of the recurrent hernia defect. A preperitoneal flap is developed, staying within the preperitoneal plane. The surgeon mobilizes the peritoneum to ensure coverage for re-peritonealization with an uncoated mesh. The subxiphoid plane is palpated for orientation. A large preperitoneal pocket is visible, and the defect is measured at three by six centimeters using surgical instruments. OV-lock absorbable sutures are prepared on a CT-I equivalent needle, designed to pass through an eight millimeter trocar.

The pneumoperitoneum is reduced to eight millimeters of mercury during the closure of the primary defect and placement of the mesh. A fifteen by fifteen centimeter sheet of proline soft mesh is deployed into the preperitoneal space. Secure straps are used to attach the mesh to the abdominal wall, avoiding placement high in the abdomen. The mesh is then re-peritonealized, resembling the fitting of a jigsaw puzzle.
